paper_title: Offline Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Q-Learning
paper_abstract:
Offline reinforcement learning requires reconciling two conflicting aims:
learning a policy that improves over the behavior policy that collected the
dataset, while at the same time minimizing the deviation from the behavior
policy so as to avoid errors due to distributional shift. This trade-off is
critical, because most current offline reinforcement learning methods need to
query the value of unseen actions during training to improve the policy, and
therefore need to either constrain these actions to be in-distribution, or else
regularize their values. We propose an offline RL method that never needs to
evaluate actions outside of the dataset, but still enables the learned policy
to improve substantially over the best behavior in the data through
generalization. The main insight in our work is that, instead of evaluating
unseen actions from the latest policy, we can approximate the policy
improvement step implicitly by treating the state value function as a random
variable, with randomness determined by the action (while still integrating
over the dynamics to avoid excessive optimism), and then taking a state
conditional upper expectile of this random variable to estimate the value of
the best actions in that state. This leverages the generalization capacity of
the function approximator to estimate the value of the best available action at
a given state without ever directly querying a Q-function with this unseen
action. Our algorithm alternates between fitting this upper expectile value
function and backing it up into a Q-function. Then, we extract the policy via
advantage-weighted behavioral cloning. We dub our method implicit Q-learning
(IQL). IQL demonstrates the state-of-the-art performance on D4RL, a standard
benchmark for offline reinforcement learning. We also demonstrate that IQL
achieves strong performance fine-tuning using online interaction after offline
initialization.

Stabilizing off policy q learning via bootstrapping error reduction
Conservative q learning for offline reinforcement learning
Controlling overestimation bias with truncated mixture of continuous distributional quantile critics
Batch reinforcement learning
Awac Accelerating online reinforcement learning with offline datasets
Advantage weighted regression Simple and scalable off policy reinforcement learning
Reinforcement learning by reward weighted regression for operational space control
Learning Complex Dexterous Manipulation with Deep Reinforcement Learning and Demonstrations

findings: Theoretical Guarantee of In-Support Optimality: A proof that as τ→1 the τ-expectile of a bounded random variable approaches its supremum (Lemma 1), and that under expectile-based DP updates the learned V_τ(s) is monotonically non-decreasing in τ (Lemma 2). Consequently, in the limit τ→1 the IQL value function converges to the optimal in-support Bellman value Q^*(s,a), establishing that IQL recovers the best possible policy constrained to dataset support.

Importance of Multi-step Dynamic Programming in Offline RL: Empirical demonstration that single-step policy evaluation methods (fitting Q^{π_β} and then greedy extraction) fail in tasks that require 'stitching' suboptimal trajectories (e.g., ant maze, toy U-maze). In contrast, IQL’s iterative expectile-based DP propagates value over multiple steps and solves these tasks, highlighting the necessity of multi-step updates.

State-of-the-Art Performance on D4RL Benchmarks: Extensive experiments on the D4RL benchmark show that IQL matches or exceeds prior offline RL methods on MuJoCo locomotion tasks and significantly outperforms both one-step and prior multi-step algorithms on the challenging Ant Maze tasks. IQL also demonstrates high computational efficiency, completing 1M gradient updates in under 20 minutes on a single GPU.

Online Fine-tuning Capability: When initialized from offline IQL and further trained with 1M steps of online interaction, IQL-based policies improve significantly, outperforming AWAC and CQL baselines on Ant Maze and dexterous manipulation tasks, demonstrating strong compatibility with standard off-policy RL finetuning.

techniques.name: Implicit Q-Learning (IQL)
techniques.description: Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) is an end-to-end offline reinforcement learning algorithm that performs multi-step dynamic programming while strictly avoiding Q‐value queries at out‐of‐support actions by alternating between two stages. In the first stage, IQL performs TD‐style learning entirely on dataset actions by fitting a state‐value network V_ψ(s) via asymmetric least squares expectile regression to the τ‐expectile of the learned Q‐values, minimizing the loss L_V(ψ)=E_{(s,a)∼D}[L_2^τ(Q_{θ̂}(s,a)−V_ψ(s))], where τ∈(0.5,1) controls the degree of implicit maximization over in‐support actions. It then fits a Q‐network Q_θ(s,a) by minimizing the mean squared error loss L_Q(θ)=E_{(s,a,s')∼D}[(r(s,a)+γ V_ψ(s')−Q_θ(s,a))^2] to the one‐step Bellman target, with γ denoting the discount factor. Both V and Q updates employ clipped double Q‐learning—maintaining two Q‐networks and using the minimum of their outputs for value targets and policy weights—and use soft target updates via Polyak averaging to update the target network Q_{θ̂}, with gradient steps taken under learning rates λ_V and λ_Q, respectively. In the second stage, IQL extracts a policy π_φ(a|s) by performing advantage‐weighted regression, optimizing L_π(φ)=E_{(s,a)∼D}[exp(β(Q_{θ̂}(s,a)−V_ψ(s))) log π_φ(a|s)], where β≥0 controls policy sharpness. This two‐stage procedure yields a policy that maximizes the learned Q‐function subject to an implicit in‐support constraint, without ever evaluating Q on unseen actions.

code.implementation:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, Tuple
from dataclasses import dataclass

# Type aliases for readability
Array = jnp.ndarray
Params = Dict[str, Array]
InfoDict = Dict[str, Any]

@dataclass
class Batch:
    """
    A batch of offline RL transitions.

    Attributes:
        observations: shape (batch_size, obs_dim)
        actions: shape (batch_size, act_dim)
        rewards: shape (batch_size,)
        next_observations: shape (batch_size, obs_dim)
        masks: shape (batch_size,)  binary mask for episode continuation
    """
    observations: Array
    actions: Array
    rewards: Array
    next_observations: Array
    masks: Array

class FunctionModel:
    """
    A simple parametric function approximator supporting SGD updates.

    Attributes:
        params: dictionary of parameters (weights, biases, etc.)
        apply_fn: function(params, *inputs) -> outputs
        lr: learning rate for SGD
    """
    def __init__(self, params: Params, apply_fn: Callable[..., Any], lr: float = 1e-3):
        self.params = params
        self.apply_fn = apply_fn
        self.lr = lr

    def apply(self, *inputs: Any) -> Any:
        """
        Apply the model to inputs using current parameters.
        """
        return self.apply_fn(self.params, *inputs)

    def apply_gradient(
        self,
        loss_fn: Callable[[Params], Tuple[Array, InfoDict]]
    ) -> Tuple["FunctionModel", InfoDict]:
        """
        Compute gradients of loss_fn w.r.t. params, perform an SGD step,
        and return the updated model plus auxiliary info.
        """
        (loss_value, info), grads = jax.value_and_grad(loss_fn, has_aux=True)(self.params)
        new_params = jax.tree_util.tree_map(lambda p, g: p - self.lr * g, self.params, grads)
        return FunctionModel(new_params, self.apply_fn, lr=self.lr), info

def expectile_loss(diff: Array, expectile: float) -> Array:
    """
    Compute the asymmetric squared loss for expectile regression:
        L₂^τ(u) = |τ - 𝟙(u < 0)| * u²

    Args:
        diff: array of (prediction - target).
        expectile: τ in (0,1), with τ>0.5 emphasizing positive diffs.

    Returns:
        Per-sample expectile losses.
    """
    weight = jnp.where(diff > 0, expectile, 1.0 - expectile)
    return weight * (diff ** 2)

class NormalDist:
    """
    A diagonal Gaussian distribution for continuous actions.
    """
    def __init__(self, mean: Array, log_std: Array):
        self.mean = mean
        self.log_std = log_std
        self.std = jnp.exp(log_std)

    def log_prob(self, x: Array) -> Array:
        """
        Compute log-probability of x under N(mean, std²).
        Returns shape (...,).
        """
        var = self.std ** 2
        log_probs = -0.5 * (((x - self.mean) ** 2) / var + 2 * self.log_std + jnp.log(2 * jnp.pi))
        return jnp.sum(log_probs, axis=-1)

def update_value(
    critic: FunctionModel,
    value_model: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    expectile: float
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    Perform expectile regression update on the value network V_ψ.

    Args:
        critic: FunctionModel returning (Q1, Q2).
        value_model: FunctionModel returning V.
        batch: Batch of transitions.
        expectile: τ for asymmetric weighting.

    Returns:
        Updated value_model and info dict with 'value_loss' and 'v_mean'.
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        v_pred = value_model.apply_fn(params, batch.observations).squeeze(-1)
        q1, q2 = critic.apply(batch.observations, batch.actions)
        q_min = jnp.minimum(q1, q2).squeeze(-1)
        loss_v = expectile_loss(q_min - v_pred, expectile).mean()
        return loss_v, {'value_loss': loss_v, 'v_mean': v_pred.mean()}

    return value_model.apply_gradient(loss_fn)

def update_q(
    critic: FunctionModel,
    target_value: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    discount: float
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    TD-update both Q-networks to r + γ V(next_s).

    Args:
        critic: FunctionModel returning (Q1, Q2).
        target_value: FunctionModel for computing V(next_s).
        batch: Batch of transitions.
        discount: γ discount factor.

    Returns:
        Updated critic and info dict with 'critic_loss', 'q1_mean', 'q2_mean'.
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        q1_pred, q2_pred = critic.apply_fn(params, batch.observations, batch.actions)
        v_next = target_value.apply(batch.next_observations).squeeze(-1)
        td_target = batch.rewards + discount * batch.masks * v_next
        loss_q = ((q1_pred.squeeze(-1) - td_target) ** 2 +
                  (q2_pred.squeeze(-1) - td_target) ** 2).mean()
        return loss_q, {
            'critic_loss': loss_q,
            'q1_mean': q1_pred.mean(),
            'q2_mean': q2_pred.mean()
        }

    return critic.apply_gradient(loss_fn)

def update_actor(
    actor: FunctionModel,
    critic: FunctionModel,
    value_model: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    temperature: float,
    clip_value: float = 100.0
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    Advantage-weighted behavioral cloning update of the policy π_φ.

    Args:
        actor: FunctionModel returning (mean, log_std).
        critic: FunctionModel returning (Q1, Q2).
        value_model: FunctionModel returning V(s).
        batch: Batch of transitions.
        temperature: β sharpness.
        clip_value: max weight to avoid overflow.

    Returns:
        Updated actor and info dict with 'actor_loss', 'adv_mean'.
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        v_pred = value_model.apply(batch.observations).squeeze(-1)
        q1_pred, q2_pred = critic.apply(batch.observations, batch.actions)
        adv = jnp.minimum(q1_pred, q2_pred).squeeze(-1) - v_pred
        weights = jnp.exp(temperature * adv)
        weights = jnp.minimum(weights, clip_value)
        mean, log_std = actor.apply_fn(params, batch.observations)
        dist = NormalDist(mean, log_std)
        logp = dist.log_prob(batch.actions)
        loss_pi = -jnp.mean(weights * logp)
        return loss_pi, {'actor_loss': loss_pi, 'adv_mean': adv.mean()}

    return actor.apply_gradient(loss_fn)
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create a random batch
    key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
    obs_dim, act_dim, batch_size = 4, 2, 8
    key, *subkeys = jax.random.split(key, 7)
    observations = jax.random.normal(subkeys[0], (batch_size, obs_dim))
    actions = jax.random.normal(subkeys[1], (batch_size, act_dim))
    rewards = jax.random.uniform(subkeys[2], (batch_size,))
    next_observations = jax.random.normal(subkeys[3], (batch_size, obs_dim))
    masks = jax.random.bernoulli(subkeys[4], p=0.9, shape=(batch_size,)).astype(jnp.float32)
    batch = Batch(observations, actions, rewards, next_observations, masks)

    # Define simple linear apply_fns
    def critic_apply_fn(params: Params, obs: Array, act: Array) -> Tuple[Array, Array]:
        q1 = obs @ params['w1_obs'] + act @ params['w1_act'] + params['b1']
        q2 = obs @ params['w2_obs'] + act @ params['w2_act'] + params['b2']
        return q1, q2

    def value_apply_fn(params: Params, obs: Array) -> Array:
        return obs @ params['w_v'] + params['b_v']

    def actor_apply_fn(params: Params, obs: Array) -> Tuple[Array, Array]:
        mean = obs @ params['w_mu'] + params['b_mu']
        log_std = params['log_std']
        return mean, log_std

    # Initialize random parameters
    params_critic = {
        'w1_obs': jax.random.normal(subkeys[5], (obs_dim, 1)),
        'w1_act': jax.random.normal(subkeys[5], (act_dim, 1)),
        'b1': jnp.zeros((1,)),
        'w2_obs': jax.random.normal(subkeys[6], (obs_dim, 1)),
        'w2_act': jax.random.normal(subkeys[6], (act_dim, 1)),
        'b2': jnp.zeros((1,)),
    }
    params_value = {
        'w_v': jax.random.normal(key, (obs_dim, 1)),
        'b_v': jnp.zeros((1,)),
    }
    params_actor = {
        'w_mu': jax.random.normal(key, (obs_dim, act_dim)),
        'b_mu': jnp.zeros((act_dim,)),
        'log_std': jnp.zeros((act_dim,)),
    }

    # Instantiate models
    critic = FunctionModel(params_critic, critic_apply_fn, lr=1e-3)
    value_model = FunctionModel(params_value, value_apply_fn, lr=1e-3)
    target_value = FunctionModel(params_value, value_apply_fn, lr=1e-3)
    actor = FunctionModel(params_actor, actor_apply_fn, lr=1e-3)

    # Perform one update step for each component
    new_value, info_v = update_value(critic, value_model, batch, expectile=0.8)
    new_critic, info_q = update_q(critic, target_value, batch, discount=0.99)
    new_actor, info_pi = update_actor(actor, critic, value_model, batch, temperature=0.1)

    print("Value Update Info:", info_v)
    print("Q Update Info:", info_q)
    print("Actor Update Info:", info_pi)
code.documentation:
This module implements the core components of Implicit Q-Learning (IQL): expectile‐regression value fitting, clipped double Q‐learning, and advantage‐weighted behavioral cloning for policy extraction. It provides:
- `Batch`: a structure for offline transitions.
- `FunctionModel`: a wrapper for parameterized functions with SGD updates.
- `expectile_loss`: asymmetric squared loss to train the state‐value network.
- `update_value`: value network update via expectile regression on min‐Q.
- `update_q`: TD‐learning for two Q‐networks using the Bellman target.
- `update_actor`: policy update with exponentiated, clipped advantages under a Gaussian actor.

All neural components are represented by simple linear functions in this example; real use would substitute MLPs or other architectures. The included TEST BLOCK demonstrates a complete update step on synthetic data, ensuring self‐containment and reproducibility.

components.name: Expectile Regression
components.description: Expectile regression estimates the τ-expectile m_τ of a random variable X by solving the asymmetric least‐squares problem m_τ = argmin_m E[L₂^τ(X − m)], where the loss function L₂^τ(u) = |τ − 𝟙(u < 0)| u² applies an asymmetric weight to residuals. In the conditional setting, given samples (x,y) ∼ D, one fits a function m_τ(x) by minimizing E_{(x,y)∼D}[|τ − 𝟙(y − m_τ(x) < 0)|(y − m_τ(x))²], thereby recovering standard mean‐squared error when τ = 0.5 and placing progressively greater emphasis on large positive residuals for τ > 0.5. As τ → 1, the estimated expectile converges to the supremum of X’s support. This objective admits unbiased stochastic gradient estimates and can be implemented simply by modifying the standard mean‐squared‐error loss in any modern machine‐learning library with the weight factor |τ − 𝟙(u < 0)|, and empirical comparisons indicate that this asymmetric squared‐loss formulation often outperforms quantile regression with its corresponding ℓ₁ loss.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np

def expectile_loss(residuals: np.ndarray, expectile: float = 0.8) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute the asymmetric squared loss for expectile regression.

    This loss corresponds to L₂^τ(u) = |τ - 𝟙(u < 0)| * u², where u = residuals.
    It applies a higher weight to positive residuals when expectile > 0.5
    and to negative ones when expectile < 0.5.

    Parameters:
    -----------
    residuals : np.ndarray
        Array of residuals (predictions minus targets) for each sample.
    expectile : float, optional
        The τ parameter in [0, 1] determining the asymmetry.
        - τ = 0.5 yields the standard mean-squared error.
        - τ > 0.5 emphasizes under-predictions (positive residuals).
        - τ < 0.5 emphasizes over-predictions (negative residuals).
        Default is 0.8.

    Returns:
    --------
    losses : np.ndarray
        The per-sample asymmetric squared losses.
    """
    # Create a boolean mask where residual > 0
    positive_mask = (residuals > 0).astype(residuals.dtype)
    # Weight = expectile for positive residuals, (1 - expectile) for negative ones
    weights = expectile * positive_mask + (1.0 - expectile) * (1.0 - positive_mask)
    return weights * (residuals ** 2)
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example usage: compute expectile loss on a batch of residuals
    np.random.seed(0)
    # Simulate residuals: predictions minus targets
    residuals = np.random.randn(10)  # 10 random residuals
    tau = 0.7  # expectile level

    losses = expectile_loss(residuals, expectile=tau)
    print("Residuals:", residuals)
    print(f"Expectile Losses (tau={tau}):", losses)
    print("Mean Expectile Loss:", losses.mean())
code.documentation: This module implements the core asymmetric squared‐loss function for expectile regression. The function `expectile_loss` takes an array of residuals and a scalar expectile parameter τ, returning per-sample losses weighted by |τ − 𝟙(residual < 0)|. When τ = 0.5, it reduces to the standard mean-squared error; values of τ > 0.5 place more emphasis on underestimations (positive residuals), and τ < 0.5 on overestimations. The test block demonstrates how to compute this loss on random residuals and prints the individual and mean losses. This loss can be directly used in gradient-based optimization loops in any numerical computing framework.

components.name: In-sample Dynamic Programming via Expectile Regression
components.description:
In-sample Dynamic Programming via Expectile Regression employs a two-step value and Q-learning procedure that never evaluates the learned Q-function on unseen actions by using τ-expectile regression.  In the first step, a value network V_ψ(s) is trained to match the τ-expectile of the target Q̂(s,a) values over dataset actions by minimizing the asymmetric squared loss L₂^τ(u)=|τ–𝟙(u<0)| u², yielding the objective  
L_V(ψ)=E_{(s,a)∼D}[ L₂^τ(Q̂(s,a)–V_ψ(s)) ],  
where Q̂ denotes a separate soft-updated target network for added stability.  In the second step, the Q-network Q_θ(s,a) is updated by minimizing the mean squared Bellman error on dataset transitions,  
L_Q(θ)=E_{(s,a,s′)∼D}[ (r(s,a)+γ V_ψ(s′)–Q_θ(s,a))² ],  
thus using only observed transitions and the learned value estimates.  Under assumptions of unbounded function capacity and as τ→1, the expectile regression asymptotically approximates the maximum over in-support actions, and this two-step procedure converges to the in-support Bellman optimal Q-function.

components.name: Advantage-Weighted Regression (AWR) Policy Extraction
components.description:
Advantage-Weighted Regression (AWR) Policy Extraction proceeds by taking as input a converged Q-function Q_{ĥθ}(s,a) and an estimate of the state value V_ψ(s), and then fitting a policy π_φ(a|s) via the supervised objective  
L_π(φ) = – E_{(s,a)∼D}[exp(β (Q_{ĥθ}(s,a) – V_ψ(s))) log π_φ(a|s)],  
where β ∈ [0,∞) is an inverse temperature that interpolates between behavior cloning for small values and Q-maximization for large values.  In practice, φ is updated by stochastic gradient descent according to  
φ ← φ – λ_π ∇_φ L_π(φ)  
over multiple gradient steps, with λ_π denoting the policy learning rate.  Crucially, all expectations and gradients are taken over state–action pairs drawn from the offline dataset D, so no out-of-support actions are ever queried.  This procedure yields a policy that maximizes the learned Q-values subject to an implicit Kullback–Leibler constraint to the data distribution, as established by prior work on advantage‐weighted regression.

components.name: Clipped Double Q-Learning
components.description: Clipped Double Q-Learning maintains two independent action-value networks, Q₁ and Q₂, and during both the value-function fitting and policy-extraction phases uses the minimum of their outputs to form targets. Specifically, when computing the expectile regression target for the value network, the algorithm evaluates r(s,a)+γ V̂(s′) by taking V̂(s′)=Eτ[a′∼μ(·|s′)][min (Q₁(s′,a′), Q₂(s′,a′))], and when constructing the advantage-weighted regression (AWR) weights for policy learning, it uses exp (β(min (Q₁(s,a), Q₂(s,a))−Vψ(s))) to reweight behavior actions. By clipping the update targets to the lower of the two Q estimates, this approach mitigates overestimation bias inherent in single-estimator methods and promotes more stable convergence throughout training.

code.implementation:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, Tuple
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

Array = jnp.ndarray
Params = Dict[str, Array]
InfoDict = Dict[str, Any]
PRNGKey = jax.random.PRNGKey


@dataclass
class Batch:
    """
    A simple batch of offline RL transitions.
    """
    observations: Array      # shape (batch_size, obs_dim)
    actions: Array           # shape (batch_size, act_dim)
    rewards: Array           # shape (batch_size,)
    next_observations: Array # shape (batch_size, obs_dim)
    masks: Array             # shape (batch_size,) binary mask for episode ends


class FunctionModel:
    """
    A minimal function approximator wrapper that supports gradient-based updates.
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        init_params: Params,
        apply_fn: Callable[..., Any],
        lr: float = 1e-3
    ):
        """
        init_params: dictionary of JAX arrays (weights, biases, etc.)
        apply_fn: function(params, *inputs) -> outputs
        lr: learning rate for SGD updates
        """
        self.params = init_params
        self.apply_fn = apply_fn
        self.lr = lr

    def apply(self, *inputs: Any) -> Any:
        """
        Apply the model to inputs using its current parameters.
        """
        return self.apply_fn(self.params, *inputs)

    def apply_gradient(
        self,
        loss_fn: Callable[[Params], Tuple[Array, InfoDict]]
    ) -> Tuple["FunctionModel", InfoDict]:
        """
        Compute loss and gradients w.r.t. params, apply a simple SGD step,
        and return a new FunctionModel along with auxiliary info.
        """
        # Compute loss and auxiliary info; also get gradients
        (loss_value, info), grads = jax.value_and_grad(loss_fn, has_aux=True)(self.params)
        # Simple SGD update
        new_params = jax.tree_util.tree_map(lambda p, g: p - self.lr * g, self.params, grads)
        new_model = FunctionModel(new_params, self.apply_fn, lr=self.lr)
        return new_model, info


def expectile_loss(diff: Array, expectile: float) -> Array:
    """
    Compute the expectile regression loss.
    weight * diff^2, where weight = expectile if diff > 0 else (1-expectile)
    """
    weight = jnp.where(diff > 0, expectile, 1.0 - expectile)
    return weight * (diff ** 2)


def update_value(
    critic: FunctionModel,
    value_model: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    expectile: float
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    Update the value network V_psi by expectile regression on
    the clipped min of two Q-values (Clipped Double Q-Learning).
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        # v(s)
        v_pred = value_model.apply_fn(params, batch.observations).squeeze(-1)
        # min(Q1, Q2)(s, a)
        q1, q2 = critic.apply(batch.observations, batch.actions)
        q_min = jnp.minimum(q1, q2).squeeze(-1)
        # expectile loss between q_min and v_pred
        loss_v = expectile_loss(q_min - v_pred, expectile).mean()
        info = {
            'value_loss': loss_v,
            'v_mean': v_pred.mean()
        }
        return loss_v, info

    return value_model.apply_gradient(loss_fn)


def update_q(
    critic: FunctionModel,
    target_value: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    discount: float
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    Update both Q-networks by TD-learning with target:
        r + gamma * V(next_s),
    where V uses the expectile-fitted value network, and
    the critic networks are trained to minimize MSE.
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        # Q1, Q2 predictions
        q1_pred, q2_pred = critic.apply_fn(params, batch.observations, batch.actions)
        # target V(next_s)
        v_next = target_value.apply(batch.next_observations).squeeze(-1)
        # TD target
        td_target = batch.rewards + discount * batch.masks * v_next
        # MSE loss for both networks
        loss_q = ((q1_pred.squeeze(-1) - td_target) ** 2 + 
                  (q2_pred.squeeze(-1) - td_target) ** 2).mean()
        info = {
            'critic_loss': loss_q,
            'q1_mean': q1_pred.mean(),
            'q2_mean': q2_pred.mean()
        }
        return loss_q, info

    return critic.apply_gradient(loss_fn)


class NormalDist:
    """
    A simple diagonal Gaussian distribution for actions.
    """
    def __init__(self, mean: Array, log_std: Array):
        """
        mean: shape (..., act_dim)
        log_std: shape (act_dim,)
        """
        self.mean = mean
        self.log_std = log_std
        self.std = jnp.exp(log_std)

    def log_prob(self, x: Array) -> Array:
        """
        Compute the log-probability of x under N(mean, std^2).
        Returns shape (...,)
        """
        var = self.std ** 2
        log_prob = -0.5 * (((x - self.mean) ** 2) / var + 2 * self.log_std + jnp.log(2 * jnp.pi))
        return jnp.sum(log_prob, axis=-1)


def update_actor(
    actor: FunctionModel,
    critic: FunctionModel,
    value_model: FunctionModel,
    batch: Batch,
    temperature: float,
    clip_value: float = 100.0
) -> Tuple[FunctionModel, InfoDict]:
    """
    Update the policy by advantage-weighted behavior cloning,
    using weights exp(beta * (min(Q1,Q2)(s,a) - V(s))) clipped to clip_value.
    """
    def loss_fn(params: Params) -> Tuple[Array, InfoDict]:
        # baseline V(s)
        v_pred = value_model.apply(batch.observations).squeeze(-1)
        # Q-min for advantage
        q1_pred, q2_pred = critic.apply(batch.observations, batch.actions)
        adv = (jnp.minimum(q1_pred, q2_pred).squeeze(-1) - v_pred)
        # exponentiated weights
        weights = jnp.exp(temperature * adv)
        weights = jnp.minimum(weights, clip_value)
        # actor distribution and log-probs
        mean, log_std = actor.apply(batch.observations)
        dist = NormalDist(mean, log_std)
        logp = dist.log_prob(batch.actions)
        # weighted behavioral cloning loss
        loss_pi = -jnp.mean(weights * logp)
        info = {
            'actor_loss': loss_pi,
            'adv_mean': adv.mean()
        }
        return loss_pi, info

    return actor.apply_gradient(loss_fn)
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Hyperparameters
    obs_dim = 5
    act_dim = 2
    batch_size = 16
    hidden = 8
    discount = 0.99
    expectile = 0.7
    temperature = 3.0

    # Create random batch
    key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
    obs = jax.random.normal(key, (batch_size, obs_dim))
    key, sub = jax.random.split(key)
    next_obs = jax.random.normal(sub, (batch_size, obs_dim))
    key, sub = jax.random.split(key)
    acts = jax.random.normal(sub, (batch_size, act_dim))
    rews = jax.random.uniform(sub, (batch_size,))
    masks = jax.random.bernoulli(sub, p=0.9, shape=(batch_size,)).astype(jnp.float32)
    batch = Batch(obs, acts, rews, next_obs, masks)

    # Helper to init linear layer params
    def init_lin(k, in_dim, out_dim):
        k1, k2 = jax.random.split(k)
        w = jax.random.normal(k1, (in_dim, out_dim)) * 0.1
        b = jnp.zeros((out_dim,))
        return w, b

    # Initialize critic: two linear Q-networks
    key, k1, k2, k3, k4 = jax.random.split(key, 5)
    w1, b1 = init_lin(k1, obs_dim + act_dim, 1)
    w2, b2 = init_lin(k2, obs_dim + act_dim, 1)
    critic_params = {'w1': w1, 'b1': b1, 'w2': w2, 'b2': b2}

    def critic_apply(params: Params, o: Array, a: Array) -> Tuple[Array, Array]:
        inp = jnp.concatenate([o, a], axis=-1)
        q1 = jnp.dot(inp, params['w1']) + params['b1']
        q2 = jnp.dot(inp, params['w2']) + params['b2']
        return q1, q2

    critic = FunctionModel(critic_params, critic_apply, lr=1e-2)

    # Initialize value network: linear
    key, kv = jax.random.split(key)
    wv, bv = init_lin(kv, obs_dim, 1)
    value_params = {'wv': wv, 'bv': bv}

    def value_apply(params: Params, o: Array) -> Array:
        return jnp.dot(o, params['wv']) + params['bv']

    value_net = FunctionModel(value_params, value_apply, lr=1e-2)

    # Initialize actor: linear mean + log_std
    key, ka = jax.random.split(key)
    wa, ba = init_lin(ka, obs_dim, act_dim)
    log_std = jnp.zeros((act_dim,))
    actor_params = {'wa': wa, 'ba': ba, 'log_std': log_std}

    def actor_apply(params: Params, o: Array) -> Tuple[Array, Array]:
        mean = jnp.dot(o, params['wa']) + params['ba']
        return mean, params['log_std']

    actor = FunctionModel(actor_params, actor_apply, lr=1e-2)

    # Perform one update of each component
    critic, info_q = update_q(critic, value_net, batch, discount)
    print("Q-update info:", info_q)

    value_net, info_v = update_value(critic, value_net, batch, expectile)
    print("Value-update info:", info_v)

    actor, info_pi = update_actor(actor, critic, value_net, batch, temperature)
    print("Actor-update info:", info_pi)
code.documentation: This module implements Clipped Double Q-Learning, encapsulating the core updates for critic (two Q-networks), value (expectile regression), and actor (advantage-weighted behavioral cloning) under the “min‐Q” clipping rule. The FunctionModel wrapper abstracts parameterized networks with simple SGD updates using JAX autodiff. The `update_q` function performs TD‐learning by regressing both Q‐functions to the one‐step target r + γ V. The `update_value` step fits the value network by expectile regression on the clipped minimum Q. The `update_actor` step uses exponentiated, clipped advantages from min‐Q and V to weight behavior cloning of the policy via a diagonal Gaussian. A test block generates random linear models and synthetic batch data to demonstrate a single update of each component. All functions are type‐annotated and documented to facilitate reuse and extension.
